rules.description: When working with Logic Apps or Power Automate flow JSON files, ensure your workflow follows this standard structure:
Power Automate supports advanced expressions in conditions to check multiple values. When working with complex logical conditions, use the following p
rules.condition: using expressions in power automate conditions
using expressions in power automate conditions

rules.description: | `greater` | Checks if first value is greater than second | `@greater(item()?['Due'], item()?['Paid'])` |
rules.condition: using expressions in power automate conditions

rules.description: | `less` | Checks if first value is less than second | `@less(item()?['dueDate'], addDays(utcNow(),1))` |
rules.condition: using expressions in power automate conditions

rules.description: | `empty` | Checks if object, array or string is empty | `@empty(item()?['Status'])` |

directives.description: These instructions will guide you in writing high-quality Azure Logic Apps and Microsoft Power Automate workflow definitions using the JSON-based Work
Use appropriate trigger types based on your scenario:
Request trigger: For synchronous API-like workflows
Event-based triggers: For reactive patterns (Service Bus, Event Grid, etc.)
Use pagination settings for high-volume data sources
Name actions descriptively to indicate their purpose
Organize complex workflows using scopes for logical grouping
